topic: A person finds out their partner has been learning their native language
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the apartment was suddenly deafening, punctuated only by the frantic drumming of my own pulse. I found the evidence tucked inside a book I hadn't noticed before—a series of meticulously organized flashcards, filled with characters and vocabulary from my grandmother's dialect. My jaw tightened so hard I thought my teeth might crack. When Liam finally walked in, whistling softly, I didn't look up. "What is that?" I managed, my voice dangerously flat. He froze, his smile dissolving instantly. The air thickened, heavy with unspoken accusations. I stood up, pacing toward the window, my hands clenching into fists at my sides. "You’ve been studying my mother tongue?" The words came out sharp, laced with a brittle edge that made my own throat feel constricted. I watched him, waiting for the inevitable defense, but all I saw was a flicker of something—guilt, maybe—and it only fueled the burning sensation rising in my chest.

[story 2]

I stared at the textbook spread across the coffee table, the script swimming before my eyes. It was the local dialect, the one I spoke with my parents, the language I carried in my bones. A cold, hard knot formed in my stomach, tightening with every second I let my gaze linger on the unfamiliar script. When Sarah finally admitted it, her explanation sounding flimsy and rushed, I felt a sudden, intense wave of heat wash over me. I dropped the remote control onto the rug with a sharp clatter. My breathing hitched, shallow and quick. I walked over to her, my movements stiff and deliberate, stopping inches away. "You’ve been learning *my* language?" My voice was low, a strained whisper that held more force than a shout. I could feel the blood rushing to my face, a flush spreading from my neck up to my hairline. I shook my head slowly, the movement jerky, unable to process the betrayal or the sheer audacity of the secret.

[story 3]

The discovery hit me like a physical blow. I picked up the notebook, the paper feeling suddenly flimsy and mocking in my hands. Every word was a direct violation, a secret language being absorbed by someone who was supposed to understand everything about me. I dropped the notebook onto the floor, the sound echoing in the quiet living room. I turned on the lights, flooding the room with harsh, unforgiving brightness. Liam looked up, his eyes wide with apprehension, sensing the seismic shift in the atmosphere. I didn't speak immediately. Instead, I moved toward the kitchen, gripping the edge of the counter until my knuckles were white. When I finally returned, my tone was clipped, every syllable precise and cutting. "Tell me why," I demanded, my posture rigid, radiating a profound sense of being deeply wronged. The way I held my arms across my chest spoke volumes—a clear boundary drawn in the sand.
